does: This paper introduces YFPO, a neuron-guided preference optimization method that improves mathematical reasoning in language models by leveraging internal neural activations as reward signals. The core contribution is using attention-based layer-wise relevance propagation (AttnLRP) to identify neurons associated with mathematical capabilities, then constructing auxiliary rewards based on the activation margin of these neurons between preferred and dispreferred responses. Rather than relying solely on external preference labels, YFPO treats neuron activation patterns as intrinsic indicators of whether the model is engaging mathematical reasoning capabilities. The method augments standard preference optimization by combining sample-level preference signals with neuron-level internal activation patterns, creating a dual supervision approach that grounds learning in the model's learned representations of mathematical knowledge and logical reasoning.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of preference optimization methods for post-training large language models, extending techniques like DPO and similar frameworks that have shown success in improving reasoning abilities. It connects to neuron interpretation research that seeks to understand which model components encode specific capabilities or knowledge domains. The approach relates to prior work on using internal model representations for training signal construction, as well as studies examining how transformer networks develop specialized attention patterns for mathematical reasoning. YFPO combines insights from preference learning with mechanistic interpretability, specifically the line of work using attribution methods like layer-wise relevance propagation to identify task-relevant neurons. This bridges the gap between external behavioral supervision and intrinsic model structure.
enables: This preliminary approach opens directions for tighter integration between interpretability and training, where internal model representations actively guide optimization. Future work could refine neuron identification methods, potentially developing more sophisticated ways to detect capability-specific neurons beyond mathematical reasoning. The framework suggests investigating whether similar neuron-guided approaches could improve performance across other reasoning domains or knowledge areas. It enables exploration of whether auxiliary rewards derived from internal activations can reduce dependence on large external preference datasets or improve sample efficiency. The method points toward developing more interpretable training procedures where learning is grounded in understood model mechanisms rather than purely empirical preference signals, potentially advancing toward more controllable and trustworthy AI systems.
